items.q: Why did the factory system replace the domestic system?
items.correct: Water-powered then steam-powered machinery was too large and expensive for cottages; factories concentrated workers around machines, enabling discipline, supervision, and scale.
items.misconception: Factories appeared because steam engines were invented.
items.random_wrong: Factories appeared because the government banned home weaving.
items.why: The shift was about machinery scale and capital investment, not just invention. Domestic spinning continued; factories triumphed where mechanisation outscaled hand work.

items.q: How did Hitler come to power in 1933?
items.correct: He was legally appointed Chancellor by President Hindenburg as part of a conservative political deal, after the Nazis became the largest party in the Reichstag.
items.misconception: Hitler seized power through a violent coup or revolution.
items.random_wrong: Hitler won an outright majority in the 1932 presidential election.
items.why: The 1923 Beer Hall Putsch failed; in 1933 Hitler came to power constitutionally. Students confuse Nazi violence with the actual mechanism of takeover, which was legal manoeuvring.

items.q: What did the Truman Doctrine commit the US to in 1947?
items.correct: Supporting free peoples resisting communist takeover — initially Greece and Turkey — formalising the policy of containment articulated by George Kennan.
items.misconception: The Truman Doctrine declared war on the Soviet Union.
items.random_wrong: The Truman Doctrine ordered the dropping of the atomic bomb on Japan.
items.why: Containment was political and economic, not military invasion of the USSR. Students conflate Cold War rhetoric with hot war; Truman's doctrine was a framework for indirect rivalry.
The answer examiners want is: George Stephenson. “James Watt” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Where was the 1829 locomotive contest held?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Rainhill. “Liverpool” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Stephenson's Rocket win at Rainhill?
items.why: The real figure is 1829. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
